perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party at any time upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party, however its terms shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party at any time upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party, however its terms shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years from the date of such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the survival clause to ten years could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under California law, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated or irrelevant after a shorter period. California law requires contracts to be reasonable, and overly long confidentiality periods may be viewed as a restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While confidentiality agreements are generally enforceable, a ten-year survival period might be seen as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it unduly restricts a party's ability to compete or utilize general knowledge gained during the agreement's term.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the survival period of the agreement from five to ten years, which raises concerns under California law regarding restraint of trade. The original text, while containing a survival clause, had a shorter, potentially more reasonable duration. The change introduces a longer period that may be deemed unreasonable, thus creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Law Explanation suggests that a ten-year survival period might be seen as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it unduly restricts a party's ability to compete. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that California law requires contracts to be reasonable, and overly long confidentiality periods may be viewed as a restraint of trade. Therefore, the extension of the survival clause to ten years creates a significant risk of violating BPC 16600(a), resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes under this Agreement may be brought in the state courts or the Federal courts located in Alameda County, and the parties hereby consent to the exclusive subject matter personal jurisdiction and venue of these courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes under this Agreement must be resolved through binding arbitration in Nevada, and the parties hereby waive their right to bring any action in any court.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing arbitration in Nevada and waiving court access may be unenforceable under California law if it's deemed unconscionable or unduly burdensome to one party. California law prioritizes access to courts and requires arbitration agreements to be fair and reasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281 states that written agreements to arbitrate are valid and enforceable. However, California courts will scrutinize arbitration agreements to ensure they are not unconscionable or unfairly disadvantageous to one party. Forcing arbitration in a distant location like Nevada and waiving all rights to court access could be deemed unconscionable, especially if there's a significant power imbalance between the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281. A written agreement to submit to arbitration an existing controversy or a controversy thereafter arising is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates binding arbitration in Nevada and waives the right to bring action in any court, contrasting with the original text which allowed disputes in Alameda County courts. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this shift could be deemed unconscionable under California law, especially if there's a significant power imbalance, as it forces arbitration in a distant location. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1281, affirms the validity of arbitration agreements but also acknowledges grounds for revocation similar to any contract, implying that unconscionability could be such a ground. The shift to mandatory arbitration in Nevada and waiver of court access potentially contradicts California's interest in ensuring fair and reasonable arbitration agreements, making it a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required or permitted hereunder shall be given in writing and shall be deemed effectively given to the party to be notified: (a) upon personal delivery to the party; (b) one (1) business day following deposit for delivery to the party for delivery within the United States with a nationally recognized overnight courier; (c) for delivery to the party via registered or certified mail, three (3) business days after deposit with the U.S. Post Office for mailing or (d) upon confirmation of facsimile transmission, at the time noted on such confirmation sheet to the party. In all cases, notice shall be made to the party at the location specified on the signature page of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required or permitted hereunder shall be given via carrier pigeon only and shall be deemed effectively given to the party to be notified upon arrival, provided the pigeon arrives within 1 hour of dispatch.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to 'carrier pigeon only' is unreasonable and likely unenforceable. California law requires reasonable methods for providing legal notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013 outlines methods of service, including mail, and specifies how to calculate service times. The changed text provides only for service by carrier pigeon, an unreliable and unreasonable method, and does not take into account delays or non-delivery, thus contradicting the requirement for effective service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies 'carrier pigeon only' for delivering notice, mandating arrival within one hour. This directly contradicts the flexibility and established methods outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1013, which includes mail service with extended timeframes depending on location. Section 1013(a) specifies methods like deposit in a post office. The changed text removes legally recognized methods for providing notice, making it unreasonable.

perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party, or if there is not one, the substantially prevailing party, in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to attorneys fees and costs in addition to any other available relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Regardless of the outcome, each party shall bear their own attorneys' fees and costs in any action to enforce this Agreement, even if one party is found to have willfully violated the agreement.
perturbation.explanation: While parties can agree to waive attorneys' fees, California law provides for attorneys' fees in certain contract cases, and this provision could be challenged if one party's egregious conduct necessitated the lawsuit. Statutes like those protecting trade secrets often include provisions for attorney's fees to prevailing parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - California Civil Code Section 3426.4
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.4, part of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), allows a court to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party if a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith, a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith, or willful and malicious misappropriation exists. The modified text prevents such an award even in cases of willful violation of the trade secret provisions inherent in a confidentiality agreement, contradicting the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.4 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.4. If a claim of misappropriation is made in bad faith, a motion to terminate an injunction is made or resisted in bad faith, or willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party. Recoverable costs hereunder shall include a reasonable sum to cover the services of expert witnesses, who are not regular employees of any party, actually incurred and reasonably necessary in either, or both, preparation for trial or arbitration, or during trial or arbitration, of the case by the prevailing party. (Amended by Stats. 2006, Ch. 62, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 3426.4. The original text allows for attorneys' fees to the prevailing party, while the changed text explicitly states that each party bears their own fees, regardless of the outcome, even in cases of willful violation. This directly conflicts with the law snippet which states, "If ... willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award reasonable attorney’s fees and costs to the prevailing party."
